text: The rain had been falling for four days straight and the windows were gray and the house smelled like damp socks and they moved from room to room without doing anything in any of them
The streetlights came on early in the afternoon and gave everything an orange tint that made the sidewalk look older and the puddles look like oil, and nobody was walking anywhere
He opened the refrigerator and stood there looking at the shelves and nothing appealed to him and he closed it and sat back down at the table where the newspaper was still folded from yesterday
The office fluorescents buzzed at a frequency that was not quite a sound and the vending machine hummed and the clock on the wall ticked and none of it added up to anything alive
She looked out the window at the parking lot and the sky was the same color as the asphalt and the cars were the same color as the sky and the whole world had been drained through a gray filter
The houseplant on the sill had dropped another leaf and he picked it up and put it in the trash with the others and did not replace the water in the saucer
They ate cereal for dinner again, standing at the counter, the spoon clinking against the bowl the only sound in the apartment, and when it was empty they rinsed it and went to the couch
The laundry sat in the dryer for the third day and the wrinkles had set in permanently and she looked at it and closed the door and walked away
November had settled into the city like a guest who would not leave, and the trees were bare and the gutters were clogged with wet leaves and the daylight lasted from nine to four and no longer
He watched the pigeons on the ledge outside the window and they watched him back and neither party moved for a long time and the radiator clanked and the room stayed the same temperature
The café was nearly empty and the music was too quiet to hear the words and the barista wiped the counter in slow circles and the espresso machine sat silent between orders that did not come

She scrolled through her phone contacts and could not think of a single person she wanted to call, not because she disliked anyone but because the effort of conversation felt like lifting something heavy with no handle

The fog had come in overnight and stayed and everything beyond thirty feet was a suggestion, the trees becoming shapes and the shapes becoming nothing, and he drove to work at twenty miles an hour seeing only what was close
They turned on the television for noise and let it play to an empty room while they lay in bed looking at the ceiling where a water stain had been spreading slowly since October
The Christmas lights on the neighbor's porch had been up since last year and half the bulbs were out and the ones that remained blinked at different rates, unsynchronized and tired
He made coffee and forgot about it and found it cold an hour later and poured it out and did not make another cup because the process of waiting for it to brew seemed to take longer than it was worth
The playground was empty in the drizzle and the swings moved slightly in the wind as if remembering children, and the sand was dark and pocked with rain and the slide was beaded with water

She sat in the car in the grocery store parking lot for fifteen minutes before going in, not because she was gathering herself but because the distance between the car and the entrance felt unreasonably far
The Sunday paper sat on the porch getting wet and he watched it through the glass door and thought about bringing it in and then thought about not bringing it in and the paper got wetter

Everything in the break room was beige — the walls, the counter, the coffee, the mood — and the overhead light had a flicker that nobody had reported because reporting it would require caring about the light
The dog lay on the floor with its chin on its paws and followed her with its eyes as she moved from the kitchen to the living room and back, and its tail did not lift
He opened his email and there were forty-seven messages and he read the subject lines of each one and replied to none and closed the laptop and the screen went dark and stayed dark
The flowers on the table had dried out without anyone noticing and now their petals were curled and brown and dropping onto the placemat in small crisp pieces that nobody swept up
She walked to the bus stop in the drizzle without an umbrella because the umbrella was in the closet and the closet was upstairs and the stairs were more than she wanted to manage this morning
The hotel room had a view of another hotel and the curtains were the color of oatmeal and the bedspread was the same color and the carpet was a shade darker and outside it was raining on a Tuesday

They canceled the plans they had made because the thought of showering and dressing and driving there and being around people and driving back felt like a full day of work for something that was supposed to be fun
The fish tank in the dentist's office was cloudy and the single fish drifted in a slow oval and the waiting room magazines were from March and the receptionist looked at her computer without typing
He sat on a bench in the park but the bench was wet so he stood up and his pants were wet and he stood there with wet pants looking at the wet bench and the wet grass and the wet sky
The battery on her phone died and she did not look for the charger because nothing on the screen had been interesting enough to keep the phone alive for anyway
The meeting lasted an hour and covered nothing new and they all filed out into the hallway where the carpet was industrial gray and the ceiling tiles had a stain in the shape of no particular thing
He ate lunch at his desk and the sandwich was fine and the chips were fine and the water was room temperature and the whole meal was an act of fueling rather than eating
The wind had died sometime during the night and the air was still and heavy and the flag in the yard hung limp against the pole like something that had given up waving
She put on socks and then sat on the edge of the bed with the shoes in her hands for several minutes, looking at them, before putting them back on the floor and lying down again
The dishwasher needed to be unloaded and the trash needed to go out and the mail was piling up on the counter and they looked at all of it and filled a glass of water instead and sat down
The commute took the same forty-two minutes it always took and the radio played the same rotation of songs and the traffic moved and stopped and moved in a rhythm that had no music in it

He noticed the fern in the bathroom was turning yellow at the tips and thought he should move it closer to the light but the light in the apartment was thin and indirect no matter where anything stood

The vending machine was out of everything except the flavor nobody wanted and she put in her coins and pressed the button for it anyway because choosing nothing felt worse than choosing the wrong thing
Sunday afternoon stretched out flat and featureless like a road through the plains, no landmarks, no turns, just hours between lunch and sleep with the windows darkening by degrees

They opened the curtains and closed them again because the light outside was the same gray as the light inside and the act of opening them had changed nothing except proving that it was the same everywhere
He brushed his teeth and looked at his face in the mirror and the mirror had a smudge on it and he did not wipe it off and the face behind the smudge did not ask him to
